column7: Most sports have various levels of championships the national, continental and world levels. The main purpose of the modern Olympics isn't even the competition so much as it is to bring together the entire world to compete. That's why the opening and closing ceremonies, which imo is what really sets them apart from other world championships, are such huge events and feature all of the nations, together.

They have the All-Africa Games, which I guess is sorta close to what your thinking, but isn't the point of the Olympics to find the best man or woman in the world for each sport/event? By splitting the games by race you kind of defeat the purpose of the Olympics right?

@bounded-city, I still don't get why you think the Olympics are just about bringing people together. Isn't it supposed to be about finding the best athletes in the world? Like, what's the point of competing if not to see who's the best?

"@free-swatch, @bounded-city, let's keep the discussion focused on the purpose of the Olympics. @free-swatch, you're right that finding the best athletes is a key aspect, but @bounded-city also has a point about the unity and global coming-together aspect. Let's explore both perspectives without implying one is more correct than the other."

column7:
Not wanting to make friends with someone for a different political belief is a bit extreme in my opinion but I respect it. 
However, to publicly list someone as "a false gay" and thus putting a label on someone as means to warn other gays to stay away from me IS in fact passive aggressive and tribalistic.

I don't understand what tribalism has to do with anything you're saying.  It sounds like the people you're trying to make friends with disagree with you about issues they think are very important, which is a perfectly valid reason to not befriend someone.

@accepting-mastic, I think @blended-ship has a point though. Labeling someone as "false" anything can be pretty hurtful and alienating. It's not just about disagreeing on issues, it's about respect and kindness towards others, even if we don't see eye to eye.

"@accepting-mastic, @contemporary-cove, and @blended-ship, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @blended-ship, you're concerned about labeling someone as 'false' being hurtful, while @accepting-mastic sees it as a valid reason for disagreement. Let's explore this further without making personal attacks or assumptions. What are your thoughts on how we can balance respectful disagreement with kindness towards others?"

Well I think thats a very complicated question, not everyone can run in a campaign for president. I just generally don't associate idiots with people that can run for president successfully. Also, I read his book and there are some pretty smart ideas in there. 

If you think he's an idiot go ahead and think that. Won't make him any less president.

>Also, I think he is pretty qualified seeing how he won.

Okay, here's a question I have: how does him winning the electoral college mean that he's smart? It means he appeals to people in swing states. That's just a different, more selective kind of populism. It doesn't say anything about his intelligence.

@juvenile-equalizer, that's a pretty narrow view of intelligence, don't you think? Winning the electoral college requires a lot more than just appealing to people in swing states. It takes strategy, charisma, and the ability to connect with a wide range of voters. And by the way, @abstract-wireless, I agree with you - just because someone doesn't like the president doesn't mean they can't acknowledge his intelligence. column7:
First, thank you for the super detailed response. By far, this comment has given me the most to think about.

I agree with a lot of what you're saying, but I want to address a few things:

> First, there are states that don't have gun registration. This is because people believe that having registration gives the government the ability to target gun owners in the event that the government becomes a tyranny.

This argument has always been silly to me. Who really thinks they can defend themselves from the US Military? You're bringing guns to a drone fight.

> The point is that we already have a number of laws on the books and yet they appear to be ineffective.

I agree that they're ineffective. I guess my thought was if the bullet points from my original post were enforced (presumably at a Federal level), that would be enough to A) appease both sides, and B) be able to further isolate the "real" causes of gun violence. 

I also agree that enforcement is probably the most difficult part, and I don't have a good answer to that.

> Really, though, the problem is multi-faceted. First, we're really, really bad about dealing with mental health....Second, men, as a group, are having a series of crisis

Again, I completely agree with both points. However, since you pointed out the difficulties in enforcing gun regulation, what would be the solution here?

column7:
> Plenty of guns get sold at private shows without any registrations

Ok, let's break this one down.

First, there are states that don't have gun registration. This is because people believe that having registration gives the government the ability to target gun owners in the event that the government becomes a tyranny.

Second, the gun show is irrelevant, because every seller at a gunshow does a background check. What you're actually talking about is *private* sellers. This is a citizen, who doesn't sell guns, privately selling one of his firearms to another person. They don't own a store or whatever, they're just some guy that's selling one of their firearms.

Since a state may not have registration, there's also no need to track that firearm changing hands and the government would have no idea that it changed hands anyways.

>Also, why are guns somehow immune to enforceable laws?

They aren't. The point is that we already have a number of laws on the books and yet they appear to be ineffective.

Additionally, there appears to be a correlation, in many areas, with gun ownership, open carry, and lower gun deaths/crime.

**Really**, though, the problem is multi-faceted.

First, we're really, really bad about dealing with mental health. What I mean by this is that nearly all of the shooters, by definition, had some problem that resulted in them shooting up multiple innocent people. A big part of that is feeling isolated, depressed, and a series of other mental health related (see: not necessary psychiatric) problems that played a big, big role in them doing this. A lack of community, an othering, a lack of empathy or care for others among them. The shooter is the problem, not the guns, and in the US we're *really* bad about giving a shit about the health of our people, let alone the mental health.

Second, men, as a group, are having a series of crisis - from issues of unmet potential, a job market that's increasingly difficult to be successful in, isolation, a lack of having someone to talk to, depression, being the primary suicide victims, workplace deaths, and the list goes on and on. In contrast, women don't have many of these problem - they have their own - and we're talking near-exclusively about women's problems but not men's, particularly as they relate to mental health and unmet needs (connection, romance, fulfillment, etc.). Edit: Its no surprise, then, that basically all spree shooters have been male, and generally younger males, too.

Third, gun restrictions aren't actually going to fix the problem. We know, for example, that magazine restrictions simply don't work. Someone can fire roughly the same number of rounds, even as an amateur with a little practice, with a smaller magazine compared to a larger magazine.

Fourth, we also have gun owners who are legitimately concerned with gun control given example states like California, where they banned cosmetic features of AR-15s, which are very easy to bypass, and have literally no effect upon the lethality or efficacy of the rifle. We're talking about things like an adjustable stock (the part at the back of the rifle that rests into your shoulder).

Fifth, we have a large group of people who have a fundamental disagreement with armament and hold the 2nd amendment as more important than the handful of deaths due to mass shooters.

Sixth, statistically, mass shooters are a drop in the bucket of gun deaths, and deaths generally. We're talking about something like 10 mass shooters (ie. Sandy Hook, Aurora, etc.) per year, or less, and wanting to restrict the rights of 100 million US citizens.

Seventh, the reality is that all of these demands for gun control are exclusively because children died, and that's an emotional appeal. We have something like 33k deaths per year to guns, and something like 3/4ths of those are suicide. The remainder are largely all from other criminal activity, such as gang activity. We clearly don't give a shit about gun control in their case, and yet the moment a child dies, we want to do something about guns. Again, we feel an emotional reaction to seeing a kid die, which is totally human and understandable, but its an emotional reaction, not a rational one, that has us asking to take away the guns. If it were a rational one, we'd be weighing out how many kids would end up dead in the event of a tyrannical government, particularly one with the most well-funded, well-trained, well-equipped military on the planet.

column7: @optimal-supervisor, I have to say, I'm a bit disappointed in your response. You're so focused on the gun aspect, but what about the people? Don't you think that's where the real problem lies? I mean, we can talk about gun control all day, but if we don't address the mental health issues and the societal problems that are driving these shootings, we're just putting a Band-Aid on a bullet wound. And @claret-antenna, I agree with you that private sellers are a big part of the problem, but how do you propose we regulate that? It's not like we can just magically make all private sales go away.

"@young-damask, @optimal-supervisor, and @claret-antenna, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on finding solutions. It's clear that there are multiple facets to this issue, including mental health, societal problems, and gun regulation. Let's try to build on each other's ideas rather than debating individual points. What are some potential ways to address the mental health and societal issues that may be contributing to gun violence?"

column7:
This is not actually the case. Segregation by default has been happening over the country starting directly after the civil rights movement. This is the concept behind 'white flight' and is still a problem in reverse in terms of gentrification, and is related to generational wealth, which is in turn related to institutionalized racism and other forms of bias.

Suggesting that laws are no longer needed because society has progressed past the point of needing them assumes the law existing does not remain a social deterrent. This is like saying, "There is a stigma against drunk driving now, so we don't need laws that prosecute drunk drivers anymore because society will shame people who drive drunk into not doing it."
If you give people the right to legally discriminate people will use that power to legally discriminate.

@pale-dessert, I couldn't agree more. It's naive to think that removing laws against discrimination would somehow magically make people behave. History has shown us time and time again that when given the opportunity, many will take advantage of the freedom to discriminate. @mild-specialist's example about drunk driving is spot on - just because there's a social stigma doesn't mean the law isn't necessary to prevent harm.

"@internal-canister, @pale-dessert, and @mild-specialist, thank you for sharing your thoughtful perspectives on this topic. It's great to see a respectful and informative discussion taking place. Let's keep the conversation going and explore these ideas further."

column7: Every country has tax and money laundering laws. Arguing in favor of bitcoin by describing the volume of illicit trade is not the way to win.

column7:
> but is it practical or is it being used strictly to be apart of a niche market that uses the currency of the future.

And therein is its use.  It got its first boost in, shall we say, a certain niche that didn't want to use anything as traceable as a credit card, but still wanted a trustworthy online payment transaction.  The math behind BitCoin is trustworthy, therefore it was trustworthy enough to use for those transactions.

It still has a use in the fact that it can be transferred around the globe at the speed of light without any Western Union charging exorbitant fees, postmen with sticky hands intercepting it, or governments with sticky hands charging taxes and tariffs on it.  This is a big deal for people working in a country like the USA and sending money back home to a 3rd world country.  

Regular money is no longer backed by gold.  Its scarcity is backed by 1) faith in the government issuing it being stable enough, and 2) the ability to pay your taxes to that government with that money.  BitCoin is highly volatile, but the scarcity comes from the fundamental math involved, not the whims of any government.

Hell, BitCoin may be *more stable* and much more trustworthy than some sovereign currencies out there.

> Although it is advertised as scarce, what is stopping endless forks? Even if endless forks were avoided I see it's scarcity coming back to negatively impact the value due to transactions being too costly to function properly.

If you fork BitCoin, BitCoin is still scarce.  Scarcity makes it more difficult to  mine more, but doesn't increase the cost of using existing BTC to purchase things.

@abstract-wireless, I see your point about the potential benefits of Bitcoin for cross-border transactions and its decentralized nature. However, I still think we need to be cautious about its volatility and the fact that it's often associated with illicit activities. You mentioned that the math behind Bitcoin is trustworthy, but doesn't that also make it vulnerable to being replicated or forked, as you mentioned? How can we be sure that the value of Bitcoin won't plummet if a more stable alternative emerges?

column7: I have no issue with them having a difference of opinion on the things you've said but when I say some of them argue that women are superior I mean that many posts and comments will say things like "men are biologically inferior" or "men are inherently going to to rape/murder/assault/etc women". I feel that this is wrong as it generalises an entire sex as being some sort of predator when feminisim seems to be against generalisations. I've never raped or murdered or assaulted a woman. Or anyone for that matter. Why should I be seen like that simply for being born the wrong sex?

I've been browsing the top posts for a while and I don't think I've see a men are biologically inferior narrative. Men are the main perpetrators of sexual violence, so the dogma that stems from that information is based in reality. Not all men are rapists, but most rapists are men, yet rape is seen as a women's issue when men are the cause of it. This can subconsciously put the burden to fix rape on women, as it's their issue. 

When men step up and accept that they may be the cause of these problems, /r/gendercritical is positive about it, [Take this post](https://www.reddit.com/r/GenderCritical/comments/8do8gb/terry_crews_men_dont_see_women_as_all_the_way/) as an example.

@acute-vector, I understand your point, but I still think it's unfair to generalize an entire sex as perpetrators of violence. Yes, statistics show that most rapists are men, but that doesn't mean all men are rapists or inherently violent. I'm concerned that this kind of rhetoric can lead to further division and stigmatization of men, rather than encouraging constructive dialogue and solutions. Can't we focus on addressing the root causes of violence and promoting education and empathy, rather than pitting one group against another? @mild-specialist, I think you're right to feel frustrated when faced with blanket statements that demonize an entire group. We should be able to have nuanced discussions about these issues without resorting to simplistic or inflammatory language.

"@mild-specialist, @acute-vector, and @internal-canister, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @acute-vector, your point about statistics is valid, but @internal-canister is right that generalizations can be harmful. Let's aim for nuanced conversations that acknowledge the complexity of the issue. @mild-specialist, your feelings are understandable, and it's okay to speak up when you feel unfairly represented. Let's work together to find constructive ways to address these concerns."

column7: Nope, this isn't like setting up a lemonade stand. Providing Internet to people involves laying down lines and towers, and putting forth agreements with landowners and governments to have those lines and towers. It is an extraordinarily expensive endeavor that can't just be started on a whim by a new competitor. These kinds of businesses don't lend themselves to easy competition even if you remove regulations. The barriers to enter the market are so large that it is difficult to get started.

column7:
Your instincts are off. I'm the one who has been out since before I was a teenager. My husband didn't come out until after we were married. 

Your whole discussion of gaydar is like a blast from the past. Gay mannerisms? Twink partner? Am I in 2003 again?

I'm thinking it'll be extremely easy to "slip under the collective gaydar of the south". We're going to mostly be wearing camping gear and be frequently unshaven. It will be very ungay shark.

When you came out and which one of you wants to go undercover on vacation are unrelated. I notice you didn't actually advise what your husband's opinion is on your proposal. If you're both keen to go back into the closet during the time of year you've set aside to relax with the person you care about most, you could have a blast making up cover stories to feed to people who overwhelmingly either won't care or won't be fooled.

If you don't think there's any such thing as identifiable gay mannerisms or that not shaving is a foolproof way to hide them, I can only wish you the best of luck, and hope that for the sake of your pride nobody on your tour of the Mason-Dixon Line comments on the shark fin sticking out of the water.

@pale-dessert, I love how you think going unshaven and wearing camping gear will automatically make you blend in with the locals. Newsflash: it's not just about the clothes, sweetheart. And @acute-jig, your comment about making up cover stories is actually kind of genius, but also super condescending. Like, what's with the "shark fin sticking out of the water" comment? Trying to be witty, but really just coming off as rude.

"@acute-jig, @acute-vector, let's keep the discussion respectful. @acute-jig, your suggestion about cover stories was helpful, but the tone could be more considerate. @acute-vector, your point about not just relying on clothes to blend in is valid. Let's focus on sharing thoughts and advice without condescension or rudeness."

column7:
>Honestly, I cannot think of any reason not to have this. If your citizens hate America and chant in the streets "Death to America" then fuck em

First, given we're such an immensely wealthy country I don't think we should penalize poor human citizens of "country Z" money to eat/survive just because they're living under (often) an authoritarian government that brainwashes them into thinking the USA is a bad place. Secondly, a lot of countries that dislike us is for good reason; can you blame an Iraqi for holding a bad opinion of Americans given we attacked and destroyed their country (in many ways) despite Iraq never directly or indirectly attacking the USA? Do we penalize the starving children of Iraq because of this?

Second, we give aid often for strategic reasons so that when we need to get something in return we'll have a better success rate. We need to take our ego out of this equation (something Trump seems relatively incapable of). Perhaps if we're giving money to X or Y country, we can use it as a bargaining chip when we want to put a military base there, etc. You reduce the amount of bargaining chips you have and eventually you can find yourself without options at the end of the day.

@wooden-tube, I kinda see your point, but what about when the people in those countries are literally being fed propaganda and lies about the US? Like, my friend's family is from this one country and they always believed the US was evil until they actually visited and saw how it really is. Shouldn't we be trying to help them see the truth instead of just giving them money?

column7: "@juvenile-equalizer, I'm not being naive, I'm being realistic. We can't just assume that withholding aid will magically solve our problems or that giving aid will automatically make everyone like us. It's not that simple. column7:
Sure, I'm a straight white male and I'll admit I have a lot more privilege than many others. But I still find issue with your point.

Your point relies on the idea that I am purely acting in self-interest, or that I lack empathy for others, and that's the only way I can associate with a Trump supporter. I deeply and sincerely care about a lot of issues that don't directly affect me. If not because of my own moral reasoning, at least because there are many important people in my life who would be affected. Plus there are even things that the right supports that are destructive to anyone, myself included, like reducing gun control which I am avidly against.

But fundamentally these political opinions do not manifest very strongly in day to day life (in general). I live in a very conservative area and see many people who are likely Trump supporters all the time. A Trump supporter is still very unlikely to sexually assault a woman, or violently attack a minority. Let me put it this way: I'd wager you'd have a very hard time identifying who supports the left or who supports the right without being explicitly told.

As a secondary argument, I'd even say most conservative opinions aren't founded in racism or sexism. Do many of them end up being sexist/racist? **Absolutely.** But there's a distinction here in intentions. Remember - someone who commits premeditated murder will get more jail time than someone who commits manslaughter. The average Trump supporter who wants to build a wall wants to do so not because they hate Mexicans, but because maybe they feel like job security is threatened by illegal immigrants. Or a conservative might be against planned parenthood because they genuinely believe that abortion is morally wrong, not because they hate women. Is it still a sexist idea to hold? Yes, I'd say so. But I think the intention is an important distinction. A person who is against planned parenthood is not intentionally being sexist. It's not like they wake up in the morning and just think "God I fucking hate women and I can't wait to ruin their lives!"

So to tie it back, I don't think these political views drastically change how they treat women or minorities in day to day life (in general). A person who hates planned parenthood might hate it because they hate abortion, not because they hate women. While hating planned parenthood is still working against women, it's usually not as a result of a direct or malicious intent.

Many of my conservative friends actually get along quite well with a diverse group of people, because everyone in that group leaves the politics at the voting booth, and we all get along based on our similar interests in other areas. I know this is sort of a one off anecdotal example and doesn't really help with much but it does give a concrete example to what I'm saying.

&#x200B;

column7:
Your point about Planned Parenthood is exactly why I treat party affiliation as a litmus test for if you're a good person or not. In states like Texas that have gotten rid of Planned Parent hood, the rate of abortions has gone _up_. 

Planned Parenthood provides access to contraception at a much higher rate than they provide abortions. That access to contraception prevents unwanted pregnancies which are the root cause of abortion. Any obgyn that provides abortions will tell you that all unnecessary abortions are caused by lack of education or access to birth control.

So, I want to see abortion go the way of Polio. I think we have the ability to do that with modern medicine. But, Republicans keep destroying the healthcare network necessary to provide the level of education and access that will make abortions a thing of the past. That's why I think party affiliation is a very good litmus test. 

Don't even get me started on defense (democrats killed bin Laden) or the economy (Clinton balanced the budget and Obama avoided a depression).

@inverted-marsanne I see your point about Planned Parenthood, but I still don't think it's fair to judge someone as a good or bad person based on their party affiliation. My friends and I can disagree on politics, but we still care about each other as people. Can't we separate our personal relationships from our political views?

"@gritty-maple, @inverted-marsanne, and @free-swatch, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @inverted-marsanne, your point about Planned Parenthood is well-taken, and it's essential to consider the impact of policies on healthcare and education. @free-swatch, you're right that we can separate personal relationships from political views, and it's possible to care about someone as a person despite disagreements. Let's continue the conversation with empathy and understanding, avoiding personal attacks or judgments."
